title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who grow their own food. For questions 43-52, choose from the people (A-D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Growing Your Own: Four People on Cultivating Food at Home

texts.A:
### Monica, 62, Retired Teacher
I have been growing vegetables since I retired four years ago and it has changed how I eat entirely. [46]I used to buy whatever was on offer at the supermarket without much thought, but now I plan my meals around what is in season in my garden, which has led me to eat a much wider variety of vegetables than I ever did before.[/46] The taste difference compared to shop-bought produce is striking. [49]What I would say to anyone thinking of starting is this: do not underestimate the time and effort involved, especially in the first year when everything is new and the mistakes are plentiful.[/49] It is deeply rewarding but it is not a passive hobby.
texts.B:
### Josh, 28, Urban Designer
Growing food in cities has become something of an obsession for me, both personally and professionally. [44]My allotment plots on a former factory site fed directly into the community garden designs I now produce for city councils - I understand what growing communities actually need in terms of space and infrastructure because I have experienced it firsthand.[/44] The overlap between my hobby and my work has made me a better designer. [50]There is also a powerful environmental argument: growing food locally reduces food miles, supports biodiversity, and helps manage urban heat, all of which are priorities in sustainable city planning.[/50] I think we are only just beginning to understand how transformative widespread urban food growing could be.
texts.C:
### Harriet, 41, Doctor
As a doctor, I regularly recommend outdoor physical activity to patients with anxiety, but [45]I specifically suggest growing food because it combines gentle exercise with a sense of purpose and achievement that is particularly effective for people who feel overwhelmed by stress.[/45] The growing cycle provides natural structure and patience. [47]What surprised me personally when I joined my local allotment association was how strong the community aspect became - I now have close friends I would never have met otherwise, and we look out for each other well beyond the plot.[/47] [52]I believe strongly that primary schools should dedicate time to food growing - children who understand where food comes from are far more likely to make healthy choices later in life.[/52]
texts.D:
### Oliver, 35, Chef
[43]I got into growing vegetables when I was frustrated by the quality and cost of fresh herbs at the restaurant supplier - I started with a few pots on the kitchen windowsill and gradually expanded to a full raised-bed garden at the back of the restaurant.[/43] Now much of what we serve is grown on site, which has changed our entire approach to menu planning. [48]The most satisfying moment in my week is picking ingredients that I planted myself and turning them into dishes that same evening - there is an immediacy and honesty about it that no amount of expensive sourcing can replicate.[/48] [51]My teenage son has started helping out on Saturday mornings, and watching him develop a genuine interest in where food comes from has been one of the most unexpected pleasures of the whole project.[/51]

questions.question: explains what first prompted them to start growing their own food?
questions.explanation: Oliver says he got into growing vegetables when frustrated by the quality and cost of fresh herbs at the restaurant supplier.

questions.question: explains how their food-growing hobby has directly influenced their professional work?
questions.explanation: Josh says his allotment experience fed directly into the community garden designs he produces for city councils.

questions.question: recommends growing food specifically as a way to manage stress?
questions.explanation: Harriet recommends growing food to patients with anxiety because it combines gentle exercise with a sense of purpose and achievement.

questions.question: describes how growing their own food has changed what they eat?
questions.explanation: Monica now plans meals around what is in season in her garden, leading her to eat a much wider variety of vegetables.

questions.question: mentions forming new friendships as an unexpected benefit of their food-growing activity?
questions.explanation: Harriet was surprised by how strong the community aspect became, and she now has close friends she would never have met otherwise.

questions.question: describes the most personally rewarding aspect of using produce they have grown themselves?
questions.explanation: Oliver says the most satisfying moment is picking ingredients he planted himself and turning them into dishes the same evening.

questions.question: warns that growing food requires more effort than beginners typically expect?
questions.explanation: Monica advises anyone thinking of starting not to underestimate the time and effort involved, especially in the first year.

questions.question: refers to the environmental benefits of growing food in cities?
questions.explanation: Josh mentions that growing food locally reduces food miles, supports biodiversity, and helps manage urban heat.

questions.question: shares their enthusiasm for food growing with a younger family member?
questions.explanation: Oliver's teenage son has started helping out on Saturday mornings and has developed a genuine interest in where food comes from.

questions.question: argues that children should learn about food growing as part of their education?
questions.explanation: Harriet believes strongly that primary schools should dedicate time to food growing.
